# Transcrição: 10-The Subtle Art of Sharing Oneself

- **Fonte:** 10-The Subtle Art of Sharing Oneself
- **Duração:** 11:16
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

So far, we've talked about establishing emotional connections and producing emotional responses in our audience in planned ways, in ways that we map out and craft specifically. But that's not always the case. As storytellers, as people who want to continue to make emotional appeals and seek those connections, oftentimes what we can do is something much subtler, the subtle art of sharing yourself in the world. A couple years ago, in a very strange set of circumstances, I ended up being the substitute minister at a Unitarian Universalist Church. It's odd because I'm not actually religious, but the minister at the church knew me as a storyteller. She was going to Florida and she said, would you mind leading the service? I said, I don't know how to do that. And she said, it'll be fine. I'll have someone help you with it. You can tell some stories. It'll be great. And I agreed. I say yes to everything. Now I knew when I walked into that church that the people knew I wasn't a minister and they actually knew that I didn't believe in God. I was a reluctant atheist, a person who was trying to believe in God but does not believe in God. I felt like I was walking into a space where people did not necessarily believe in me or believe in the job I was about to do. I needed to get people to emotionally connect with me as quickly as possible. I was subtle about it. I was careful about it and I was nuanced about it. The first thing you have to do whenever possible is not speak for as long as possible. I have a rule that when I'm in a room with people, I try to be the last person to speak. If I'm at a party or a dinner, I try to be the last person at the table or the last person in the group to say anything for two reasons. One is, I know I'm a storyteller. I have a big mouth. I can talk all day. So I have to control it. But more importantly, number two, I want to gather information. One of the best ways for me to emotionally connect with people, to find ways to join our lives together, is to gather as much information as possible so that I can choose to say the thing that will most likely connect with them. The more I know about each person, the more I will understand them and the better I will be at choosing a story or an anecdote or even just a comment to say to those people. So being quiet, being a listener, inventorying the information that you're receiving can be critical when you finally decide to share something of yourself. On that particular day in the church, I knew that I wanted the people in that church to know I was a husband and that I loved my wife. I had children. I grew up in Massachusetts. The church was in Massachusetts. I live in Connecticut. But I wanted the people to know I grew up in this area. I understand you. I think you'll understand me.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

So I wanted them to know I originated nearby. I grew up in this land that they live in now. I also wanted them to know that I'm an elementary school teacher. That is a very safe thing to be in this world, an honored and treasured thing. When people find out I'm an elementary school teacher, they automatically feel a little more affectionate to me. But then I listened. When I arrived at the church early, I listened to what they were saying. I discovered they were talking about the Red Sox game the night before. I'm a Yankees fan. But that doesn't mean I shouldn't talk about the Yankees because Red Sox fans and Yankee fans can emotionally connect in our rivalry. It gives us something to talk about. So at some point over the course of my church service, I was going to find a way to mention that I was a Yankees fan. I also found out that in the church, there was a cat. The church had a cat that chased and killed the mice in the church. I am a cat owner and a cat lover. Since the church has a cat, I'm going to mention I have a cat. I assume these people like their cat. All of that and more. And so I was subtle about it. I got to ring the bell at the church. It was the best part of the whole service. Big piece of rope. They told me to pull it down and then make sure I let go because if I didn't, I would go up into the chimney. And so I pulled it down and the bell rang and it was great. And as I began the service, one of the first things I said was, thank you for letting me ring the bell. My son would be so jealous of me. My son would be so jealous of me was my way of saying, I'm a parent and I'm a parent of a small kid, the kind of kid that would love to ring a big bell. It was my first signal to this congregation that I was a safe person. I was a father of children and I love my kids. A couple of minutes later, I had to rise from my seat and sort of step out in front of everybody. And when I did, I apologized for not wearing a tie. I noticed everyone in the audience was essentially wearing a tie and I was not. And I said, my wife tried to convince me to wear a tie and I always listen to my wife. She's the smartest person I know. But on this one occasion, I kind of ignored her and now I regret it. That was me acknowledging, first of all, that I have underdressed and made a joke of it. But more importantly, I identified myself as the husband of a woman and I love the woman because everything she says is usually right. Every single wife in that audience instantly loved me and probably wished that their husband was more like me. It was my way of saying, I'm married and I have kids. Throughout the rest of the service, I also managed to mention that I had two cats by referencing the cat in the church. I mentioned that I was a Yankees fan by acknowledging all the Red Sox talk prior to service. I mentioned that I grew up in a small town in Massachusetts, not unlike this one, that

## [06:03 - 09:05]

I went to church as a child in a white church on a hill, like the white church on the hill that I was standing on at that moment. All of those things were said to make significant emotional connections to these people. After the church service, we were going to have a spaghetti dinner. During the course of the service, I mentioned how much I was looking forward to the spaghetti, that I love spaghetti so that anyone who cooked that food would now be appreciative of me and then I told a bunch of stories. I couldn't get out of the place. After the spaghetti dinner, I sat at a table and people just came and started telling me their stories. They told me stories related to the stories that I had told and they told me their own stories. Through very little effort on my own, while sticking primarily to the script that I was given, I managed to weave in little bits of information, a subtle art of sharing yourself. This can be as simple as on a Zoom call, what is behind you? What is in the background? If you're using a fake background, one of those digital backgrounds, you're making a terrible mistake and frankly, everyone just assumes there's dirty laundry behind that fake background. It's like you're hiding something. Why are you doing that? Instead, take advantage of that real estate. Put a picture of your family. If your cat walks across the screen, be thrilled that your cat walked across the screen. If your child interrupts a meeting, be thrilled that your child interrupted the meeting. All of these things make us relatable. They connect us to other human beings. If you're a tennis player, hang a racket on the wall. If you're a fan of opera, put something on the wall that indicates you're a fan of the opera. If you're a golfer, put a golf ball on a table behind you in case someone in the Zoom audience is also a golfer and suddenly understands you in a way that only golfers can understand each other. We have to find ways to carefully and subtly insert ourselves, our personalities, our loves, our beliefs, the things that make us us. We have to subtly insert those into the conversations we're having and into our personal environment. We have to tell people who we are. We cannot afford to be round, white, and tasteless. We have to be people with edges and color. We have to let people know that we're interesting, unique, and varied individuals. We have to find the things that we know people love to hear. Things like, I understand what it's like to have children. I understand what it's like to be married. I understand what it's like to have a difficult boss. I know what it's like to have a great boss. I know what it's like to be waiting for my vacation. I know what it's like to have a terrible vacation. I know what it's like to walk the dog at four o'clock in the morning. I know what it's like to walk the dog at midnight. All of these things and many, many more make us interesting, unique individuals. The kind of things that people will connect to.

## [09:05 - 11:16]

You want people to look at you and not think, well, that was interesting content. What you want them looking at you and thinking is, that's a person who I understand. That person has something in common with me. That person and I share a similar belief system. Therefore, the things that person is saying are important to me. The product that person is selling means something to me because that person means something to me and it doesn't take much. It doesn't take much especially today because in today's world, people are so reticent to share of themselves. I use crowbars and hammers to convince people to say one personal thing over the course of a marketing presentation. It takes all of their effort to say, I have a daughter. I like swimming. I'm dating. It takes all of my effort to get them to say one thing. For you to say a few things, for you to be strategic and subtle, for you to share of yourself and get people to believe in you, it's not going to be hard at all because it's so hard for everyone else. I told you earlier how important it is to zig while everyone else is zagging. This is the perfect example of it. You can produce content that will be forgettable. You can say things just like everybody else said it. You can be replaceable. Your boring talk track, your ordinary talk track, your talk track like all the other talk tracks we've ever heard can be just like everybody else's or you can be something different. You can infuse yourself into things you do. Share of yourself. Be a storyteller. Share a little bit of your own life while sharing the things you need to share. Be brave. Be different. Find the things that you could share and start doing it today. Let's take the first step in that direction now.
